BharSt_3.1: May Śiva, white as light and crowned with the crescent moon, shine as the lamp of knowledge in the hearts of yogins, scattering the dense darkness of delusion.
BharSt_3.2: I have wandered through harsh lands, abandoned status, served others without reward, and eaten in humiliation like a crow at strangers' doors. Still, desire stretches itself further and is not satisfied.
BharSt_3.3: I dug the earth for treasure, smelted mountains, crossed the sea, pleased kings, and spent nights in cremation grounds for secret rites. Yet not even a broken coin came to me. Desire, be content now if you can.
BharSt_3.4: I swallowed the cruel speech of the wicked, forced smiles over hidden tears, and bowed before the rich whose minds were hardened by wealth. Hope, empty hope, what more will you make me perform?
BharSt_3.5: For the sake of keeping this frail life alive, we have even shamelessly advertised our own virtues before the rich whose minds are numbed by wealth. Desire makes people cross lines they once thought firm.
BharSt_3.6: We endured heat and cold, gave up comfort, strained the mind day and night, and did what ascetics do, but not for liberation. We chased wealth instead, and so missed the fruit of true discipline.

BharSt_3.7.en: We did not really consume pleasures; pleasures consumed us. Time did not merely pass; we ourselves passed away. Desire did not grow old; we did.
BharSt_3.7.te: మనమే భోగాలను అనుభవించలేదు; భోగాలే మనల్ని ఖర్చు చేశాయి. కాలం మాత్రమే గడవలేదు; మనమే గడిచిపోయాం. ముసలితనం కోరికకు రాలేదు; మనకే వచ్చింది.

BharSt_3.8: The face is covered in wrinkles, the head marked with grey, and the body begins to slacken. Only desire keeps insisting on staying young.
BharSt_3.9: The wish for pleasure has weakened, public regard has faded, friends have gone, eyesight fails, and the body trembles toward death. Yet still this foolish frame is afraid to let go.
BharSt_3.10: Hope is like a river filled with fantasies, agitated by waves of craving, haunted by crocodiles of attachment, and destructive to the trees of courage. Those who cross beyond it finally rest in purity.
BharSt_3.11: Looking closely at worldly life, I do not see any lasting good in it. Even the results of merit often ripen into fresh fear, because the objects won through effort turn into new burdens.
BharSt_3.12: Sense pleasures are bound to leave, whether we release them or not. If they leave on their own, they burn the mind; if we let them go ourselves, they open into the peace of renunciation.
BharSt_3.13: Those whose minds are made clear by higher knowledge can drop wealth even while they still possess it. But we, who trust neither what has gone nor what may come, cannot even release our imagined hopes.
BharSt_3.14: Blessed are those who dwell in mountain caves and meditate on the supreme light while birds drink their tears of joy without fear. Our own lives, by contrast, wear away inside mansions built from daydreams and amusement.
BharSt_3.15: Food gained by begging is meagre and tasteless, the earth is the bed, the body itself is the only companion, and torn cloth is enough for covering. Even then, sense-objects refuse to release their hold.
BharSt_3.16: The body is praised with glittering comparisons, but its actual substance is far less noble. Poets have dressed what is blameworthy in beauty; detachment begins when that spell is broken.
BharSt_3.17: Among the passionately attached, Śiva alone shines, because he made even his beloved one half of himself. Among the detached, none surpasses the one free from entanglement. The rest, pierced by desire, can neither enjoy fully nor renounce cleanly.
BharSt_3.18: The moth rushes into flame and the fish bites the hook without understanding. We, even while knowing the nets of entanglement, still fail to let go. Such is the depth of delusion.
BharSt_3.19: When thirsty we call cool water happiness, when hungry we call food happiness, and when desire blazes we call embrace happiness. People often mistake the mere relief of affliction for true joy.

BharSt_3.20.en: The deluded take house, children, wealth, beloved, and youth to be enduring, and so settle into the prison of worldly life. Blessed is the one who sees all this as fleeting and lets go.
BharSt_3.20.te: ఇల్లు, పిల్లలు, సంపద, ప్రియుడు లేదా ప్రియురాలు, యౌవనం ఇవన్నీ నిలకడైనవే అనుకునే అజ్ఞాని సంసార కారాగారంలోనే స్థిరపడిపోతాడు. ఇవన్నీ క్షణభంగురమని చూసి విడిచిపెట్టేవాడే ధన్యుడు.

BharSt_3.21: If the householder's wife is not seen in torn clothes, dragged by hungry children and broken by want, what self-respecting person would choke on the word 'give' for the sake of feeding his own burning stomach?
BharSt_3.22: The begging bowl breaks the knot of pride, cuts through false dignity, and mocks worldly ambition. Hunger exposes what status tries to hide.
BharSt_3.23: Better to carry a skull-bowl and go door to door for food while preserving one's dignity than live in daily humiliation before one's social equals for the sake of the belly.
BharSt_3.24: Have the cool banks of the Ganges and the mountain stones vanished? If not, why do people still prefer food obtained in humiliation from others?
BharSt_3.25: Have the caves lost their roots and tubers, the mountains their streams, and the trees their bark? If not, why look upon the faces of petty men, whose eyebrows dance with pride over a little wealth?
BharSt_3.26: Live now on roots and fruits, sleep on the earth softened with fresh leaves, and go to the forest, where even the names of those small-minded lords maddened by money are not heard.
BharSt_3.27: Fruit is available in every forest, cool river water can be found in many places, and soft beds of leaves are easy to make. Yet people still endure humiliation at the doors of the rich.
BharSt_3.28: When meditation breaks, I will remember with inward laughter those who suffer the pain of begging before the wealthy and those who shrink themselves to fit the minds of pleasure-bound people.
BharSt_3.29: Those who live in contentment remain inwardly joyful; those driven by greed are never free from thirst. Why admire wealth? Even Meru's golden splendor does not attract one who is complete within himself.
BharSt_3.30: Food gained by begging is free from abasement, easy, purifying, and a destroyer of pride, envy, and fear. The great yogins praise it as an inexhaustible feast given by Śiva.

BharSt_3.31.en: Pleasure carries the fear of disease, rank the fear of decline, wealth the fear of rulers, beauty the fear of old age, strength the fear of enemies, and life itself the fear of death. In this world, only detachment is fearless.
BharSt_3.31.te: భోగానికి రోగభయం, పదవికి పతనభయం, సంపదకు రాజుభయం, అందానికి ముసలితన భయం, బలానికి శత్రుభయం, జీవనానికే మరణభయం. ఈ లోకంలో నిర్భయమైనది వైరాగ్యమే.

BharSt_3.32: Birth is seized by death, youth by old age, contentment by greed, calm by passion, virtues by envy, forests by wild beasts, kings by the wicked, and prosperity by instability. What here is not swallowed by something else?
BharSt_3.33: Health is uprooted by countless disorders, wealth opens the door to calamities, and whatever is born is quickly taken over by death. What has fate truly made stable here?
BharSt_3.34: Pleasures are as unsteady as high waves breaking; life is destroyed in a moment; youth lasts only a few days. Seeing the whole world as unsubstantial, the wise turn their effort toward steady realization and the good of others.
BharSt_3.35: Pleasures flash like lightning in clouds, life breaks as easily as water on a lotus leaf, and youth is notoriously unstable. Seeing this clearly, the wise turn their minds toward yoga and steadiness.
BharSt_3.36: Life tosses like a wave, youth stays only a few days, wealth is built from imagination, pleasures shimmer like lightning, and even intimacy with the beloved does not last. Bind the mind to Brahman if you mean to cross this sea of becoming.
BharSt_3.37: In the womb one stays with difficulty amid impurity; in youth pleasure itself is tangled with the pain of separation; in old age one lives under neglect and ridicule. Tell me honestly, where in worldly life is even a little true happiness?
BharSt_3.38: Old age stands over us like a tigress, disease attacks the body like an enemy, and life leaks away like water from a cracked pot. Strange that people still choose what harms them.
BharSt_3.39: Pleasures are fragile, and worldly life revolves around them. Why keep exhausting yourself? If my words deserve trust, settle the mind into clarity and peace rather than letting it be dragged again by desire.
BharSt_3.40: There is a joy in which even the realms of Brahmā and Indra seem like blades of grass, and beside whose taste all worldly grandeur goes flat. Seek that ever-risen delight, not pleasures that vanish in a moment.
BharSt_3.41: That lovely city, that mighty king, that brilliant court, those moon-faced women, those princes and praise-songs, all have gone into memory. Salutations to Time, by whose power everything passes.
BharSt_3.42: Where many gather, one remains; where one remains, many follow; and in the end none remain. Day and night are the two dice with which Time plays on the board of the world using living beings as its pieces.

BharSt_3.43.en: With every rising and setting of the sun, life grows shorter. Yet burdened by endless tasks, people do not notice time passing, nor do birth, old age, misfortune, and death awaken real fear. The world is drunk on the wine of delusion.
BharSt_3.43.te: ప్రతి సూర్యోదయం, సూర్యాస్తమయంతో జీవితం కాస్త కాస్త తగ్గిపోతుంది. అయినా పనుల భారంతో మునిగిపోయినవాడు కాలం పోతున్నదీ గమనించడు; జననం, జరా, అపాయం, మరణం చూసినా నిజమైన భయం కలగదు. మోహమనే మద్యం తాగినట్టే ప్రపంచం మత్తులో ఉంది.

BharSt_3.44: Night returns, day returns, and creatures run again into the same repeated acts. Crushed by this recycled traffic of worldly life, we should feel shame, yet delusion keeps us unembarrassed.
BharSt_3.45: We did not meditate properly on the Lord to cut through worldly bondage, nor did we earn merit strong enough to open heaven's gate, nor even fully enjoy the pleasures people chase. We have only served as axes cutting down our mother's forest of youth.
BharSt_3.46: We mastered no noble learning that could defeat opponents, won no fame by valor, and did not even taste the sweetness of love. Youth has passed uselessly, like a lamp burning in an empty house.
BharSt_3.47: We gained no spotless learning, earned no wealth, served not even our parents with focused care, and never embraced the beloved. Time drives us on like crows after scraps from another's plate.
BharSt_3.48: Those from whom we came have gone; those with whom we grew have also passed into memory; and now we ourselves stand near the same fall, like trees on the bank of a river made of sand.

BharSt_3.49.en: A human life is measured as a hundred years, but half is spent in night, and much of the rest in childhood and old age. What remains is consumed by illness, separation, and service. Where, in a life so unstable, is lasting happiness?
BharSt_3.49.te: మనిషి జీవితం వందేళ్లేననుకున్నా అందులో సగం రాత్రుల్లోనే పోతుంది; మిగతాదిలో చాలా భాగం బాల్యం, వృద్ధాప్యంలో కలిసిపోతుంది. మిగిలిందే రోగం, వియోగం, పరసేవల్లో కరిగిపోతుంది. ఇంత చంచలమైన జీవనంలో స్థిరమైన సుఖం ఎక్కడ?

BharSt_3.50: A moment a child, a moment a youth drunk on desire, a moment poor, a moment prosperous, then old and broken like an actor changing costumes, a person finally enters the marketplace of death.
BharSt_3.51: You are a king, but we too stand tall in the dignity of learning received from teachers. Your wealth makes you famous; our poetry carries our name through the directions. If you turn away, we are still free of need.
BharSt_3.52: You command wealth; we command speech. You may be heroic in battle; we are skilled in humbling argument. The rich serve you; the thoughtful listen to us. If you have no regard for me, king, I have even less dependence on you.
BharSt_3.53: We are content in bark-cloth and you in silk; the contentment is the same. The poor one is only he whose craving is vast. When the mind rests satisfied, who is rich and who is poor?
BharSt_3.54: Fruit is enough for food, water is enough for drink, earth is enough for a bed, and bark is enough for clothing. When this is so, why submit to the arrogance of the unworthy?
BharSt_3.55: We can live on alms, wear the sky as our cloak, and sleep upon the earth. Why then should we busy ourselves with kings?
BharSt_3.56: We are neither actors, wits, singers, nor court-flatterers, nor are we women adorned for display. Why should we present ourselves before a king at all?
BharSt_3.57: This world was produced, held, and enjoyed by many great rulers before us. Why then should anyone burn with pride over owning a few towns for a little while?
BharSt_3.58: This earth has already been enjoyed, even if only briefly, by hundreds of kings. What honor is there in gaining it now? Fools rejoice over the smallest fraction of a fraction of it as if it were truly theirs.
BharSt_3.59: The whole earth is only a lump of clay encircled by a line of water, yet armies of kings fight to make it 'mine.' Shame on such men, and greater shame on those who still crave tiny gifts from them.
BharSt_3.60: Once there was a man whose skull Śiva himself placed upon his head as an ornament. Against that, what is this fever of pride in mortal men who bow only to preserve their own lives?
BharSt_3.61: Why torment your heart daily by trying in many ways to please the minds of others? If your own inner jewel awakens, what desired thing will not ripen from that inward clarity?
BharSt_3.62: Mind, why wander uselessly? Rest somewhere. What is to happen will happen as it does, not otherwise. Stop clinging to the past and stop inventing the future.
BharSt_3.63: Turn away from the exhausting thicket of sense-objects and take the path of the good, which is skilled in ending sorrow. Enter your own deepest nature, abandon your restless motion, and do not delight again in this fragile becoming.
BharSt_3.64: Wipe away delusion, cultivate love for the Lord who wears the crescent moon, and attach the heart to the banks of the heavenly river. What confidence can anyone place in waves, bubbles, lightning flashes, fortune, flame-tips, serpents, or rushing streams?
BharSt_3.65: Mind, do not trust this unstable goddess of fortune who trades in the frowns of kings. We would rather wait for alms with a bowl in hand along the streets of Vārāṇasī.
BharSt_3.66: If music, poets, attendants, and the pleasures of refined life are truly at hand, then enjoy the flavor of worldly existence. If not, mind, enter at once into thought-free absorption.
BharSt_3.67: Suppose wealth that grants every wish is obtained; what then? Suppose one stands on the heads of enemies; what then? Suppose allies are won and life is prolonged for an age; what then? What lasting answer does any of it give?
BharSt_3.68: When devotion is present, the fear of birth and death fades from the heart; attachment to relatives loosens; desire-born agitation falls away; and the solitude free of worldly contamination becomes victory itself. What higher thing is left to seek than detachment?
BharSt_3.69: So think on the infinite, undecaying, supreme Brahman instead of these unreal alternatives. Once that is seen, lordship, enjoyment, and all worldly attainments look pitiably small.
BharSt_3.70: Mind, you dive to the underworld, leap into the sky, and roam the directions in restlessness. Why not, even by accident, turn toward the pure Brahman within, where true release is found?
BharSt_3.71: What use are Vedas, law-books, Purāṇas, and sprawling systems of ritual that promise heavenly rewards, if they do not open the way into the bliss of the Self and burn away the burden of worldly sorrow?
BharSt_3.72: When Meru itself falls, seas dry up, and the earth comes to an end, what can really be said for the stability of this body, flickering like the edge of an elephant calf's ear?
BharSt_3.73: The body shrinks, the gait falters, teeth fall out, sight fades, deafness grows, the mouth drools, speech is ignored, the wife no longer serves, and even the son becomes hostile. Such is the misery of old age.
BharSt_3.74: Seeing the hair turn white, the young women withdraw from an aging man as though he were a foul outcaste well. Old age announces itself before the body is ready to accept it.

BharSt_3.75.en: While the body is still healthy, while old age is still far, while the senses retain their force, and while life has not yet run down, the wise should strive hard for the highest good. Digging a well after the house catches fire is useless.
BharSt_3.75.te: శరీరం ఆరోగ్యంగా ఉన్నప్పుడే, ముసలితనం ఇంకా దూరంలో ఉన్నప్పుడే, ఇంద్రియాలకు శక్తి ఉన్నప్పుడే, ఆయుష్షు పూర్తిగా క్షీణించకముందే జ్ఞాని ఆత్మశ్రేయస్సుకోసం ఘనంగా ప్రయత్నించాలి. ఇల్లు మంటల్లో పడిన తర్వాత బావి తవ్వటం వృథా.

BharSt_3.76: Should we dwell by the sacred river in austerity, serve noble spouses with gentleness, or drink deeply from the nectar of scripture and poetry? Human life lasts only a few moments, and we scarcely know what should be done.
BharSt_3.77: Kings are hard to please and fickle as restless horses; we too are driven by large desires; old age seizes the body and death steals life. Friend, apart from austerity, the wise have no better path.
BharSt_3.78: When honor fades, wealth breaks apart, petitioners leave disappointed, relatives diminish, attendants disappear, and youth slowly slips away, the wise should choose a cave by the Ganges rather than cling to the wreckage.
BharSt_3.79: Moonlight is lovely, forest places are lovely, the joy of good company is lovely, stories in poetry are lovely, and even the tear-bright face of a beloved in anger is lovely. Yet when the mind awakens to impermanence, none of these hold the same power.
BharSt_3.80: Palaces are pleasant, music is pleasant, and intimacy with the beloved is pleasant too. Yet seeing that all of it flickers like a lamp-shadow in the wind, the wise go to the forest.
BharSt_3.81: We have searched the three worlds and still have not found anything else that can truly restrain the proud inner elephant of the mind intoxicated by sense-objects.
BharSt_3.82: To roam freely, eat without meanness, live among fellow seekers, hear what leads only to peace, and feel the mind grow quiet: I wonder what noble austerity ripens into such a life.
BharSt_3.83: Ambitions are worn out, youth has passed, and even fine qualities go unrecognized where there are no true judges. Powerful Time arrives suddenly. Now I know there is no refuge apart from the feet of the destroyer of desire.
BharSt_3.84: Whether one worships Maheśvara or Janārdana, I do not insist on a division in ultimate reality. Yet my heart's devotion still leans toward the moon-crested Lord.
BharSt_3.85: When shall we sit upon some shining riverbank under broad moonlight, troubled by worldly life no more, repeating 'Śiva, Śiva' until tears gather thick within?
BharSt_3.86: After giving away all we have, remembering how poorly worldly fortune ripens, may we spend our nights in a holy forest beneath the autumn moon, taking refuge in contemplation of Śiva's feet.
BharSt_3.87: When will I live in Vārāṇasī on the bank of the heavenly river, wearing only a loincloth and with folded hands crying out, 'Lord of Gaurī, destroyer of Tripura, three-eyed Śambhu, be gracious,' while the days pass like moments?
BharSt_3.88: After bathing, worshipping, meditating in a mountain cave, living on fruits, and delighting in the teacher's word, when will I be freed from the sorrow that comes from serving worldly powers?
BharSt_3.89: When shall I become solitary, desireless, peaceful, using my hand as a bowl and the quarters as my garment, fit at last to uproot karma, O Śambhu?
BharSt_3.90: When the hand is a bowl, alms are enough, the world itself is a resting place, and awareness touches unbroken bliss, a kind of wealth appears that comes easily by Śiva's grace.
BharSt_3.91: If there is torn cloth for a loincloth, a ragged wrap besides, fearless alms, sleep in the cremation ground or forest, free wandering, inward peace, and firmness in yoga, what value remains in ruling the three worlds?
BharSt_3.92: For the truly high-minded, even the cosmic egg is only a small sphere. The ocean is not really disturbed by the flicker of a tiny fish.
BharSt_3.93: Mother Lakṣmī, seek someone else; do not stand longing at my door. Those attached to enjoyment may chase you. For now, leaves stitched into a bowl and the food placed in it are enough for my life.
BharSt_3.94: The earth is a great bed, the arms are a pillow, the sky is a canopy, the wind a fan, the autumn moon a lamp, and detachment a beloved companion. In such a state, the sage sleeps like a king.
BharSt_3.95: There stands an ascetic who lives on alms, moves outside the crowd, acts from inner freedom, gives up gain and loss, wears worn-out clothing from the roadside, and desires only the peace that comes from self-mastery.
BharSt_3.96: People keep asking whether such a one is an outcaste, a brahmin, a servant, an ascetic, or a master of truth. Yogins go on their way untouched, neither angered nor pleased by the talk of others.
BharSt_3.97: Food that harms no living thing is easily found, animals live contentedly on grass and lie on the ground, and for those whose minds can cross the ocean of worldly life a simple way of living has already been prepared. All qualities ripen when one seeks that path.
BharSt_3.98: Seated in meditation on a Himalayan stone by the Ganges, absorbed in yogic rest, one may become so harmless and still that old deer come without fear and scratch themselves against the body.
BharSt_3.99: The hand is a pure bowl, begged food is inexhaustible nourishment, the ten directions are a vast garment, and the broad earth is a sufficient bed. Blessed are those content within, who have cast off the whole traffic of abasement and begin to uproot karma.
BharSt_3.100: O earth, wind, fire, water, and sky, receive this final gesture of reverence. Through the merit born of dwelling with you, delusion has been dispelled, and the mind dissolves into the supreme reality.
